# Cross-Model Seeds: api-authz

Generated by: Probe Strategist (Step 4 — Cross-Pollination)
Source A: round-1-hypotheses.md (backward-reasoner-01)
Source B: round-2-hypotheses.md (contradiction-reasoner-01)

---

## CROSS-01: SearchUserGroups + LIKE Wildcard Enumeration + Low-Privilege User Privilege Mapping

Source-A: PH-03 (backward-reasoner — SearchUserGroups authenticated-only, full group enumeration)
Source-B: PH-15 (contradiction-reasoner — ListRoles GroupIDs from OIDC/LDAP claim)

Connection: Both hypotheses share the same data pathway. `SearchUserGroups` exposes all LDAP/HTTP group names to any authenticated user. `ListRoles` uses group IDs (mapped from group names at login) to determine project access. If an attacker can enumerate all group names via `SearchUserGroups`, they can identify high-privileged groups (e.g., "harbor-admins", "project-XYZ-admin") without needing to know their internal IDs. When combined with LDAP group claim injection (separate vector), the attacker knows which group names to forge in their OIDC/LDAP claims.

Combined hypothesis: An authenticated low-privilege user can (1) enumerate all LDAP group names via `SearchUserGroups` with wildcard, identifying privileged groups, then (2) if they have access to modify their LDAP attributes or OIDC groups claim, forge membership in those groups to gain elevated Harbor access. The `SearchUserGroups` endpoint serves as the reconnaissance layer for privilege escalation.

Test direction for causal-verifier: Verify that `SearchUserGroups` returns group names including LDAP group DNs (which would be even more valuable reconnaissance). Check whether `SearchByName` in `usergroup/dao/dao.go:168` includes `ldap_group_dn` in the result — the SQL selects `id, group_name, group_type, ldap_group_dn` [line 175]. YES — LDAP DNs are returned. Verify the response model exposes this field.

---

## CROSS-02: Webhook SSRF + Robot Account Creation Persistence

Source-A: PH-07 (backward-reasoner — robot accounts can create other robot accounts)
Source-B: PH-10 (contradiction-reasoner — webhook SSRF no private IP filter)

Connection: Both vulnerabilities are accessible to a project admin. A compromised project admin can (1) register an SSRF webhook to scan internal network infrastructure, and (2) create a persistent backdoor robot account that survives rotation of the original admin credentials. These are the two highest-impact findings that share the project admin trust boundary.

Combined hypothesis: A malicious project admin can establish persistent access via a shadow robot account AND use webhook SSRF to exfiltrate internal network topology (cloud metadata service credentials, internal service endpoints). The combination means: initial access via any project admin credential leads to persistent access + internal network reconnaissance. If the cloud metadata service returns IAM credentials with broader permissions, this escalates to infrastructure compromise.

Test direction for causal-verifier: Verify that webhook SSRF is triggered asynchronously (meaning the admin doesn't need to stay logged in — the job service executes the HTTP request independently). Verify that robot-created-by-robot tokens have identical permissions to the creating robot. Check if there's any parent-child tracking of robot accounts that would allow mass revocation.

---

## CROSS-03: securityhub tagFilter + CreateInClause Integer Safety

Source-A: PH-04 (backward-reasoner — securityhub filter validation via checkQFilter)
Source-B: PH-12 (contradiction-reasoner — countExceedLimit wraps sqlStr in EXISTS)

Connection: Both address the securityhub SQL construction pipeline. PH-04 concluded that user values go to `?` placeholders (safe). PH-12 identified that `tagFilter` uses `CreateInClause` which returns a literal SQL fragment `IN (id1, id2, ...)` that is APPENDED to `sqlStr`, not parameterized. This `sqlStr` then gets wrapped by `countExceedLimit`'s `fmt.Sprintf("SELECT EXISTS (%s LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1000)", sqlStr)`.

Combined hypothesis: The `tagFilter` in securityhub builds `" and a.id " + inClause` where `inClause` is `IN (id1, id2, ...)` with integer IDs from `CreateInClause`. The `orm.go:259` code uses `strconv.FormatInt(id, 10)` to convert int64 to strings. Since these are integer IDs (int64), they produce purely numeric strings — no SQL injection possible. The EXISTS wrapper is safe because the inner SQL only contains `?` placeholders for parameterized values PLUS the integer ID list from CreateInClause (which are numeric-only).

Confirmed safe: `CreateInClause` converts int64 → decimal string via `strconv.FormatInt`. Integer IDs cannot contain SQL injection characters. The EXISTS wrapping is therefore safe.

Assessment: PH-12 is INVALIDATED. The protection is real — `CreateInClause` uses integer-only formatting.

Test direction for causal-verifier: Confirm by code review that no `CreateInClause` call with a non-integer ID type exists in the codebase that could produce a non-numeric ID string.

---

## CROSS-04: Label ListLabels Global Scope Unauthenticated + Search API Unauthenticated

Source-A: PH-05 (backward-reasoner — label scope manipulation, INVALIDATED)
Source-B: PH-13 (contradiction-reasoner — ListLabels global scope no auth check)

Connection: Both address unauthenticated read access patterns. PH-13 correctly identifies that `ListLabels` for global scope has no authentication call in the method body (auth is only in the `requireAccess` helper called for CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE, not for LIST). The `ListLabels` method at `label.go:91` only calls `RequireProjectAccess` when `scope == LabelScopeProject`.

Combined hypothesis: An unauthenticated HTTP client can call `GET /api/v2.0/labels?scope=g` to enumerate all global (system-level) labels without any credentials. Global labels are visible to all users once they're authenticated, but an unauthenticated enumeration is still a meaningful gap — especially since labels are applied to artifacts and could reveal security classification tags, compliance status labels, or internal organizational naming.

Test direction for causal-verifier: Confirm by tracing the exact `ListLabels` code path — verify there is NO call to `RequireAuthenticated` or `RequireProjectAccess` for the global-scope path. Check if `Prepare` for `labelAPI` has an auth check.

---

## CROSS-05: Retention IDOR Oracle + GetRetention Load-Before-Auth Pattern

Source-A: PH-02 (backward-reasoner — retention IDOR existence oracle)
Source-B: PH-16 (contradiction-reasoner — retention scope.Reference IDOR for trigger)

Connection: Both address the "load then check" pattern in the retention API. PH-02 notes the 403 vs 404 oracle; PH-16 notes that a system admin bypasses this. Together they form a complete picture: the retention policy integer IDs are globally enumerable by any authenticated user (403 if exists in another project, 404 if doesn't exist). And system admins can READ any retention policy directly.

Combined hypothesis: Any authenticated user can enumerate which integer retention policy IDs exist across the entire Harbor instance via GET /api/v2.0/retentions/{id} — they receive 403 for existing policies they can't access, vs some error for non-existent IDs. While this only reveals existence (not content), it leaks the total count of retention policies and their ID distribution. More critically, the "system admin reads any retention policy" behavior means a compromised system admin account gains access to all retention policies, including those from projects they don't administer.

Test direction for causal-verifier: Check what error code `retentionCtl.GetRetention` returns when the ID doesn't exist vs when the ID exists but access is denied. Confirm the 403-vs-404 distinction. Verify that `requirePolicyAccess` (which validates the policy ID matches the project's metadata) is called AFTER `requireAccess` — meaning the existence oracle fires before the policy-project consistency check.
